questions.text: What is the difference in parameter update behavior between L1 and L2 weight regularization?
options: L1 applies a constant decay force regardless of parameter size, driving weights to exactly zero; L2 decays weights proportionally to their size, shrinking them without setting them to zero.
L1 shrinks weights exponentially toward zero; L2 subtracts a constant penalty from the weights on every step.
L1 regularizes bias parameters only; L2 regularizes weight parameters only.
L1 scales the weights up during training to preserve variance; L2 scales the weights down during inference.
questions.correct_answer: L1 applies a constant decay force regardless of parameter size, driving weights to exactly zero; L2 decays weights proportionally to their size, shrinking them without setting them to zero.
questions.explanation: L2 regularization subtracts a decay term proportional to the weight magnitude, which diminishes as the weight approaches zero. L1 regularization subtracts a constant rate (via the signum function) regardless of the weight's magnitude, driving small weights to exactly zero and producing sparsity.

questions.text: Why is the scaling factor 1/(1-p) applied to active activation units in Inverted Dropout during training?
options: To preserve the expected value (scale) of the activations, ensuring it is identical between the training and inference phases.
To normalize the inputs to a standard normal distribution and prevent exploding gradients.
To scale down parameters that have historically received large gradient updates.
To resolve the premature learning rate decay associated with adaptive gradient optimizers.
questions.correct_answer: To preserve the expected value (scale) of the activations, ensuring it is identical between the training and inference phases.
questions.explanation: During training with dropout probability p, a fraction p of the activations are zeroed out, which reduces the expected value of the activations by a factor of 1-p. Dividing by 1-p during training scales the active units up, keeping their expected scale normalized and matching the inference phase where dropout is turned off.
